skill.name: bats-testing-patterns
skill.description: Guidance for writing Bats tests for shell scripts, including assertions, fixtures, setup and teardown, stubs, and CI usage.
skill.summary: Build reliable Bats tests for shell scripts and CI workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell commands, filesystem operations, URLs, path traversal patterns, and weak crypto keywords. Manual review found these are Markdown examples for Bats testing, installation, fixtures, and CI usage, with no executable payload or prompt injection evidence. The skill is low risk because users may copy shell examples into projects, but no malicious intent or hidden data exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Examples in Documentation
low_findings.description: The static external command findings are in Markdown examples for installing Bats, writing tests, stubbing commands, and running CI. These are not executable skill code, but copied examples can run commands in a user project.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged commands are inside Markdown code fences and SKILL resource references. Context confirms tutorial intent, with no hidden execution mechanism in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Temporary Filesystem Operations in Test Examples
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings cover mktemp directories, fixture writes, chmod, and cleanup with rm -rf. These are common Bats testing patterns and are scoped to temporary variables, but copied examples should preserve safe variable initialization.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples create and remove temporary test directories using local variables. The risk is low but real if a user copies cleanup commands without safe setup.

low_findings.title: Relative Project Paths Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings point to Bats examples that source or run a script from a project bin directory using BATS_TEST_DIRNAME. No evidence shows user-controlled traversal or access outside the test project.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative paths are static project layout examples in test code. There is no semantic evidence of attacker-controlled path traversal.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links and Installation Source
low_findings.description: The network findings are a git clone command for the public Bats repository and reference links for documentation. No evidence found of credential collection, telemetry, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible public documentation and installation references. They do not receive local data or secrets in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings are false positives from ordinary text such as description, descriptive, or unrelated test wording. No cryptographic implementation or insecure hashing guidance was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no crypto APIs, algorithms, hashes, or security-sensitive cryptographic examples at these locations. The static pattern matched text, not code behavior.

content.value_statement: Shell scripts are hard to test because behavior depends on exits, output, files, and environments. This skill gives practical Bats patterns for reliable shell testing.
actual_capabilities: Explains Bats test file structure and common assertion styles.
Shows how to test exit codes, output, files, and error conditions.
Provides setup and teardown patterns for temporary test resources.
Covers fixtures, command stubs, function mocks, and environment overrides.
Includes examples for CI workflows, Makefiles, and shell compatibility checks.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a runnable test generator by itself.
Examples use placeholder functions that must be adapted to each project.
It focuses on shell behavior, not full system integration testing.
Users must review shell commands before running copied examples.

use_cases.title: Add Tests to Existing Shell Scripts
use_cases.description: Create Bats tests that check exits, output, file effects, and expected failures for existing scripts.

use_cases.title: Standardize CI Shell Testing
use_cases.description: Set up repeatable shell test commands for GitHub Actions, Makefiles, and parallel Bats execution.

use_cases.title: Teach Shell Test Design
use_cases.description: Use concrete patterns to help teams write isolated, readable, and maintainable shell tests.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Bats Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner test coverage for one shell command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Bats test file for this shell script. Cover one successful command, one failed command, exit codes, and output checks.

prompt_templates.title: Add Fixtures and Cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: Design Bats tests that use fixtures and temporary directories. Include setup, teardown, file assertions, and safe cleanup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Mock External Dependencies
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced isolated testing for commands that call other tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor these shell tests to stub external commands. Show how to control PATH, define stubs, and verify behavior without network calls.

prompt_templates.title: Build a CI Testing Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Bats testing strategy for this repository. Include test layout, helper files, fixtures, shell compatibility checks, and CI commands.

output_examples.input: A shell utility has no automated tests and several error cases.
output_examples.output: A structured Bats test plan covering success paths, invalid input, missing files, expected output, and exit status behavior.

output_examples.input: A script writes files and depends on temporary input data.
output_examples.output: A fixture-based testing approach with setup, teardown, isolated work directories, file assertions, and cleanup guidance.

output_examples.input: A CI pipeline needs repeatable shell script tests.
output_examples.output: A practical CI outline that installs Bats, runs test files, and reports TAP output for automated review.

best_practices: Keep each Bats test focused on one behavior and one expected outcome.
Create temporary resources in setup and remove them in teardown.
Stub external commands when a unit test should not depend on network or host state.
anti_patterns: Do not run copied shell examples without reviewing paths and variables.
Do not mix slow integration checks into fast unit test files.
Do not rely on local machine tools without skip logic or dependency checks.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps create and improve Bats tests for shell scripts, command behavior, fixtures, and CI workflows.

faq.question: Does it install Bats automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides installation examples, but users must choose and run commands for their own environment.

faq.question: Can it test scripts that write files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for temporary directories, fixtures, file assertions, and cleanup.

faq.question: Can it mock external commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes function mocks, PATH-based command stubs, and environment overrides.

faq.question: Is it only for Bash?
faq.answer: It focuses on Bats and shell scripts, with examples for bash, sh, and dash compatibility checks.

faq.question: What should users review before running examples?
faq.answer: Users should review install commands, cleanup commands, paths, and any command that changes files or PATH.
